text: The surgeon performs an incision in the right flank, preparing it for ileostomy placement at the procedure's end. An 11mm trocar is inserted, followed by a 6mm flexible trocar at the six o'clock position and another 6mm flexible trocar at the twelve o'clock position.

A bicurved grasping forceps is introduced through the flexible trocar at the twelve o'clock position, while a monocurved grasping forceps is inserted through the flexible trocar at the six o'clock position.

The small bowel is positioned above the right liver. The origin of the inferior mesenteric vein is exposed, and the surgeon uses a monocurve coagulating hook inserted at the six o'clock position to free the vein.

The surgeon introduces a straight 5mm clipper plier through the flexible port at the six o'clock position to clip the exposed inferior mesenteric vein. Monocurve scissors are also introduced through the same port for further manipulation.
The surgeon dissects the left mesocolon while carefully preserving Toldt's fascia, maintaining ergonomic positions throughout the procedure.
The surgeon frees the inferior mesenteric artery at its origin and clips it. The artery is then cut using monocurve scissors.
The surgeon dissects the left mesocolon from the gyrotous fascia, freeing the sigmoid and left colon from the lateral parietal wall.

The superior limits of the rectal mobilization are displayed from the abdominal view. The surgeon sections the left mesocolon intercorporeally using a monocurve coagulating hook and monocurve grasping forceps.
The level of colic transection is clearly visible, marking the transition to transanal laparoscopy for the continuation of the procedure.

The surgeon introduces a reusable D-port through the anus, followed by the application of a silicon cap with three orifices. A graduated straight grasping forceps is inserted through the three o'clock lumen of the cap.

The surgeon introduces a monocurve grasping forceps through the nine o'clock lumen and a monocurve needle holder with a suture through the three o'clock lumen. A purse string suture is applied underluminally, positioned two centimeters below the rectal lesion.
The surgeon performs intracorporeal suturing within the rectal cavity, demonstrating ergonomic handling of instruments without collisions between their tips.
The surgeon completes the purse string suture by performing an extracorporeal knot, which is then pushed inside the D-port. The suture is cut using monocurve scissors.

The surgeon introduces a monocurve coagulating hook into the lumen at the three o'clock position to begin the total mesorectal excision (TME). The rectal mucosa is circumferentially delimited for transection.

The surgeon performs dissection by transfixing the rectal wall, initially focusing on the posterior aspect. The dissection progresses laterally on both sides, carefully respecting the levator ani muscle plane.
The surgeon joins the two lateral dissections medially, then continues the dissection posteriorly and laterally, ensuring to stay within the correct cleavage plane.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection, separating the rectum from the prostate in male patients, or from the vagina in female patients, carefully navigating the anatomical planes between these structures.
During the lateral dissection, the surgeon exposes the inferior rectal artery and sections it using 5 mm clips.

The surgeon sections the peritoneal sheet at the level of the promontorium, connecting to the previous dissection performed via abdominal laparoscopy. The peritoneal sheets on both the left and right lateral sides of the pelvis are also sectioned.
The surgeon completes the medial sectioning of the peritoneal sheet, providing a final view of the total mesorectal excision (TME) area.
The surgical specimen is pushed into the pelvic cavity and grasped by a grasper inserted transanally.
The surgeon removes the D-port and extracts the specimen from the surgical site. The left colon is then sectioned at the previously marked segment.
The surgeon inserts a circular anvil of the stapler into the anus and performs a purse string suture. The D-port is reinserted to facilitate the procedure.

The surgeon uses a monocurve needle holder to insert a suture into the lumen at the three o'clock position, performing a purse string suture on the rectal stump by taking the rectal wall in full thickness.
The surgeon extracts the suture and creates an external knot, which is then pushed inside the D-port to secure it against the anvil of the stapler.
The surgeon cuts the suture and removes the D-port. A circular stapler is introduced into the anus to perform the anastomosis. An intraluminal check is conducted to ensure proper connection.
The surgeon closes the pelvic peritoneal sheet using a monocurved needle holder, completing the procedure.

The surgeon performs closure on the right side of the pelvis, extending to the origin of the main vessels, and secures it with an intracorporeal knot. The same closure technique is then applied to the left side.
The surgeon searches for the ileocecal valve and selects a twenty-centimeter ileal loop. The small bowel is then positioned in its natural anatomical location.
The surgeon completes the procedure by placing a temporary ileostomy at the site of the single incision.
